title: Conditional Forms: If and Then (Agar & Toh)
learningGoal: Master the construction of complex conditional sentences using 'Agar' (If) and 'Toh' (Then) across real, future, and imaginary scenarios.
instructions: Welcome to Month 3: Advanced Fluency. In this lesson, you will learn how to connect two ideas using conditionals. Pay close attention to how the tense of the verb changes depending on whether the condition is 'real' or 'imaginary'.

lessonContent.bodyText:
In English, we say 'If X happens, Y will happen.' In Hindi, we use 'Agar' (अगर - If) at the start of the first clause, and 'Toh' (तो - Then) at the start of the second clause.

Structure: [Agar + Condition], [Toh + Result].

Example:
- If you come, then I will go.
- Agar tum aaoge, toh main jaunga. (अगर तुम आओगे, तो मैं जाऊँगा।)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर बारिश होगी, तो हम घर पर रहेंगे।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar baarish hogi, toh hum ghar par rahenge.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If it rains, then we will stay at home.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर वह पूछेगा, तो मैं सच बता दूँगा।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar woh poochega, toh main sach bata doonga.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If he asks, then I will tell the truth.

lessonContent.bodyText:
When predicting a realistic future outcome, Hindi often uses the Future tense in BOTH the 'If' clause and the 'Then' clause. (Unlike English, which uses Present tense for the 'If' clause).

English: If you study (Present), you will pass (Future).
Hindi: Agar tum padhoge (Future), toh tum pass hoge (Future).

Sometimes, native speakers use the Past tense in the 'If' clause to show that the condition must be completed before the result happens.

Alternative: Agar baarish hui (Past), toh main nahi aaunga. (If it rains, then I won't come).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर तुम काम करोगे, तो पैसे मिलेंगे।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar tum kaam karoge, toh paise milenge.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If you work, then you will get money. (Both Future)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर तुम कल आए, तो हम फिल्म देखेंगे।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar tum kal aaye, toh hum film dekhenge.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If you come tomorrow, then we will watch a film. (Past + Future)

lessonContent.bodyText:
How do you say 'If I had money right now, I would buy a car'?

In Hindi, hypothetical situations use a special verb form called the 'Contrafactual'. It is formed by taking the verb root and adding 'ta', 'ti', or 'te' (exactly like the present tense, but WITHOUT 'hoon/hai/hain').

Structure: Agar [Subject + Root-ta/ti/te], toh [Subject + Root-ta/ti/te].

Example: If I went, I would see.
- Agar main jaata, toh main dekhta. (अगर मैं जाता, तो मैं देखता।)

Notice there is NO 'hai' or 'tha' at the end. The sentence just cuts off at the 'ta' ending. This abrupt ending is the universal sign of a hypothetical thought.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर मेरे पास पैसे होते, तो मैं गाड़ी खरीदता।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar mere paas paise hote, toh main gaadi khareedta.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If I had money, then I would buy a car.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर वह जानती, तो वह तुम्हें बता देती।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar woh jaanti, toh woh tumhein bata deti.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If she knew, then she would tell you.

lessonContent.bodyText:
To express regrets about the past ('If you had studied, you would have passed'), we use the Past Participle (gaya, dekha) followed by 'hota' (would have been).

Structure: Agar [Subject-ne + Verb-Past + hota], toh [Result].

Example: If I had gone, I would have seen him.
- Agar main gaya hota, toh maine use dekha hota. (अगर मैं गया होता, तो मैंने उसे देखा होता।)

Example: If you had told me, I would have come.
- Agar tumne mujhe bataya hota, toh main aa gaya hota. (अगर तुमने मुझे बताया होता, तो मैं आ गया होता।)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर हमने जल्दी की होती, तो हम ट्रेन पकड़ लेते।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar humne jaldi ki hoti, toh hum train pakad lete.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If we had hurried, then we would have caught the train.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर तुमने मुझे बुलाया होता, तो मैं ज़रूर आता।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar tumne mujhe bulaya hota, toh main zaroor aata.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: If you had called me, I definitely would have come.

lessonContent.bodyText:
In spoken Hindi, the word 'Agar' is frequently dropped. The presence of 'Toh' in the middle of the sentence is enough to let the listener know it is a conditional statement.

Example: Tum aaoge, toh main jaunga. (You come, then I will go).
Example: Paise hote, toh main de deta. (Had money, then I would give).

However, you CANNOT drop the 'Toh'. It acts as the bridge holding the two clauses together.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: वक्त मिला, तो मैं कॉल करूँगा।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Waqt mila, toh main call karunga.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: [If] I get time, then I will call.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: भूख लगी है, तो खाना खा लो।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Bhookh lagi hai, toh khaana kha lo.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: [If] you are hungry, then eat food.

lessonContent.bodyText:
To say 'Even if', we add 'bhi' (also/even) right after the condition, and replace 'Toh' with 'Phir bhi' (even then).

Structure: Agar [Condition] + bhi, phir bhi [Result].

Example: Even if it rains, I will go.
- Agar baarish hui bhi, phir bhi main jaunga. (अगर बारिश हुई भी, फिर भी मैं जाऊँगा।)

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: अगर तुम उसे बुलाओगे भी, फिर भी वह नहीं आएगा।
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Agar tum use bulaoge bhi, phir bhi woh nahi aayega.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: Even if you call him, he still won't come.

learningObjective: The learner will seamlessly connect clauses to express conditions, differentiate between future reality and past hypotheticals, and utilize the 'ta/te/ti' cutoff form for imaginary scenarios.

examples.targetText: अगर वह मेरा दोस्त नहीं होता, तो मैं उसकी मदद नहीं करता।
examples.transliteration: Agar woh mera dost nahi hota, toh main uski madad nahi karta.
examples.baseTranslation: If he was not my friend, I would not help him.
examples.explanation: Hypothetical present. Uses 'hota' and 'karta' without any 'hai' or 'tha' at the end.

examples.targetText: अगर मैं प्रधानमंत्री होता, तो मैं यह कानून बदल देता।
examples.transliteration: Agar main pradhan mantri hota, toh main yeh kanoon badal deta.
examples.baseTranslation: If I were the prime minister, I would change this law.
examples.explanation: Classic Type 2 Unreal Conditional. The sentence cuts off abruptly on 'deta'.

importantRules.ruleText: In Type 2 (Unreal) conditionals, NEVER end the sentence with 'hai', 'hoon', or 'tha'. End it directly on the 'ta/ti/te' suffix (e.g., Main jaata, woh aati).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Adding 'tha' makes it past habitual ('I used to go') instead of hypothetical ('I would go').

importantRules.ruleText: You can skip the word 'Agar' at the start of the sentence, but you MUST keep the word 'Toh' in the middle to connect the two ideas.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Dropping both makes two disconnected sentences.

cultureContext.contextText: Indian philosophy and conversation often delve into hypotheticals and fate. Phrases like 'Agar kismat mein hoga, toh milega' (If it is in destiny, then you will get it) are deeply embedded in daily life. The 'Agar... Toh...' structure is the backbone of storytelling, negotiation, and advice.
dosList: Use 'Agar... toh...' to politely threaten or bargain: 'Agar aap daam kam karenge, toh main do loonga' (If you lower the price, I will take two).
dontsList: Don't confuse 'Toh' (Then) with 'Tu' (Informal You). They sound similar to beginners but serve completely different functions.

summary: You have unlocked complex sentence structures! You can now negotiate reality ('If you do this, I will do that') and imagine alternate universes ('If I were rich, I would buy that'). Mastering the sudden stop of the 'ta/ti/te' suffix will make you sound incredibly fluent.

checkpointQuestions.questionText: If you are predicting a real future event ('If it rains, I will sleep'), what tenses do you use in Hindi?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: Future + Future (Agar baarish hogi, toh main sounga) OR Past + Future (Agar baarish hui, toh main sounga).

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What happens if you add 'tha' to the end of a hypothetical sentence (e.g., 'Agar main jaata tha...')?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: It becomes incorrect for hypotheticals. It changes the meaning to a past habit ('If I used to go') rather than an imaginary condition ('If I were to go').

checkpointQuestions.questionText: Which word can be dropped in spoken Hindi: Agar or Toh?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: Agar can be dropped. Toh is mandatory.

keyTakeaways: Type 3 (Past Regret): Agar [Past Participle + hota], Toh [Past Participle + hota].
Toh is mandatory, Agar is optional.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Mastery of conditional logic, hypothetical endings, and clause connection.
importantFacts: 'Toh' is the central pivot of all conditional sentences in Hindi.

checkpointAnswers.cq_2: It breaks the hypothetical structure. 'Tha' is for habits.

adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learners can express what will happen, what would happen, and what would have happened.

feedbackRules.onPass: Phenomenal! Understanding the 'ta/ti/te' cutoff is one of the hardest parts of Hindi grammar.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review Section 3 carefully. The secret to saying 'I would go' is literally just taking the present tense 'Main jaata hoon' and chopping off the 'hoon'.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort. Pay close attention to the difference between 'Agar main gaya' (If I go/went - real possibility) and 'Agar main jaata' (If I were to go - imaginary).

audioAssets.audioTranscript: अगर बारिश होगी, तो हम घर पर रहेंगे। अगर तुम काम करोगे, तो पैसे मिलेंगे। अगर मेरे पास पैसे होते, तो मैं गाड़ी खरीदता। अगर हमने जल्दी की होती, तो हम ट्रेन पकड़ लेते। वक्त मिला, तो मैं कॉल करूँगा। अगर तुम उसे बुलाओगे भी, फिर भी वह नहीं आएगा।

hintText: The word 'Toh' is like a seesaw pivot. The 'Agar' side balances with the 'Result' side.
commonMistakes: Using the English structure 'Agar main jaata hoon, main dekhta hoon' instead of the proper hypothetical cutoff.
